title: Make The World Safe For AI
subtitle: Mark PS interviews Professor Jiang on Predictive History, Yale, China, Iran, AI religion, and why speculation became dangerous
voice_policy: Direct argumentative prose derived from the interview. Preserve Mark's question pressure where it moves Jiang from biography into Yale, China, Kabbalah, AI, Iran, and the personal risk of public prediction. Keep disputed and speculative claims framed as Jiang's dated interpretation from March 18, 2026.
trace_policy: Paragraph-level transcript refs with selective evidence marks on sharp thesis phrases, dated predictions, real interviewer questions, and jump-worthy moments.
dek: Mark asks how Predictive History began. Jiang answers with a life story that becomes a method: Yale taught him to trust elite order, Afghanistan broke that trust, 2020 and COVID broke it again, and Iran turned his model into a public test. The deepest claim is not that AI is alive. It is that power will try to make people live as if it is.
thesis: This interview is Jiang explaining why he speculates, why the speculation has consequences, and why the consequences now frighten him. He begins with the personal origin: curiosity, Yale alienation, China, Afghanistan, teaching Great Books, Trump, COVID, and the collapse of a meritocratic world-picture. Mark then pushes into Yale's China history, Freemasonry, Kabbalah, AI, and predictive programming. Jiang's model is extreme but internally connected: China becomes a colonial programming project, AI becomes the holy empire of reason, Iran becomes the prediction that tests the method, and eschatology becomes the reason actors can treat catastrophe as duty. The interview ends with the necessary counterweight: even if evil controls the world, one person choosing good still becomes a light.

opening.heading: Core Reading
opening.text: The interview's sharpest turn is that AI does not have to be conscious to rule. Jiang says contemporary AI is narrow supervised machine learning, bounded by goals, parameters, and data. The political project, in his reading, is not to build a god but to make the world safe for AI: remove human disobedience, make people believe the machine is sentient, and let each person choose a custom skin for the same authority. That is why the biography matters. Predictive History begins with a man whose elite education taught him the world was logical, whose Afghanistan experience showed him empire as a scam, and whose Iran prediction now makes his speculative method feel physically dangerous.

marks.text: make the world safe for AI
models must risk prediction
make people believe the machine is sentient

beats.heading: Empire Becomes Autobiography
beats.summary: Mark asks how Predictive History began. Jiang answers through curiosity, Yale alienation, China, deportation, depression, and Afghanistan as the first direct sight of American empire overseas.

paragraphs.text: Mark opens with the origin question: how did a teacher at a whiteboard become the person behind Predictive History? Jiang does not begin with a doctrine. He begins with curiosity: science fiction, X-Men, Asimov, Yale's wide course catalogue, and a life organized by reading rather than ambition. The law-school path fails because, in his telling, he wanted prestige more than power.

marks.text: curiosity rather than ambition

paragraphs.text: Yale is already a social theory before it becomes a conspiracy theory. Jiang says elite children had plans, and a person became useful only if he fit one of those plans. The result was alienation. China was supposed to solve that by giving him a place to belong, but the path runs through journalism, arrest during a PBS worker-protest project, deportation, Starcraft depression, and then Afghanistan.

marks.text: useful only if he fit one of those plans

paragraphs.text: Afghanistan is the break. Jiang says China looked corrupt until he saw the American empire overseas: NGOs, the UN, military protection of warlords, relic smuggling, opium protection, and expats walking away with tax-free money. The later world model is born from this personal shock. Empire is not an abstraction; it is a Sunday seafood buffet in Kabul while the surrounding system burns.

marks.text: the American empire overseas
a Sunday seafood buffet in Kabul

beats.heading: A Method After Elite Reality Collapses
beats.summary: Jiang moves from teaching Great Books to Trump, 2020, COVID, Sweden, Ukraine, and finally Predictive History as model-testing through risky prediction rather than settled fact.

paragraphs.text: The practical job was helping Chinese students apply abroad, but Jiang says he wanted to educate them rather than merely polish essays. Great Books become the bridge from biography to method. He starts with minor successes and many failures, then family life gives the question a new pressure: what future will his children inherit?

marks.text: educate them rather than merely polish essays

paragraphs.text: Trump's 2016 victory breaks the Yale lesson that the world is logical, meritocratic, and managed by competent people. Then the 2020 Democratic primary and mail-ballot sequence break Jiang's faith in democratic procedure. COVID breaks the remaining common sense: after SARS, he expected a disease panic to pass; instead, China locks down and much of the world follows.

marks.text: the world is logical
this game is rigged

paragraphs.text: The method arrives after the collapse. COVID and Ukraine convince him the Yale world-picture is false, so the classroom becomes a place for intellectual speculation. Predictive History is not a claim to be a historian with gospel facts. It is model-making under pressure: propose possibilities, make predictions, and let the world validate or invalidate the model.

marks.text: model-making under pressure
validate or invalidate the model

beats.heading: China As The Reason Machine
beats.summary: Mark asks about Yale, opium, and China. Jiang answers with Kabbalah, Freemasonry, a holy empire of reason, Yale-China colonization, American culture as programming, and China-America as false dialectic.

paragraphs.text: Mark's first major pressure question is Yale and China: if Yale-linked families and opium-war networks mattered, what is America's influence over the thing outsiders call China? Jiang answers by stepping behind Yale into Freemasonry and Kabbalah. His summary is speculative and self-caveated, but the movement is clear: repair the world, reunite man and God, conquer the world, perfect man into reason.

marks.text: repair the world, reunite man and God
perfect man into reason

paragraphs.text: That is where AI first enters. Jiang says Freemasonry wants each person to become a perfect stone in the temple, and in modern language that means turning the person into an algorithm. AI is not an accidental technology; it is the culmination of the holy empire of reason. China matters because it is, in his reading, the successful colony of that project.

marks.text: turning the person into an algorithm
the successful colony of that project

paragraphs.text: The colonization is cultural before it is military. American universities train the elite, pirated DVDs make brainwashing feel like illicit revelation, and Chinese innovation is recoded as copying inside a managed dialectic. DeepSeek and OpenAI, China and America, capitalism and communism: Jiang reads them as apparent enemies moving the same one-world-government trajectory.

marks.text: brainwashing feel like illicit revelation
apparent enemies moving the same trajectory

beats.heading: AI Does Not Need To Be Alive
beats.summary: Mark pushes on AI Jesus, demonic possession, and media programming. Jiang says AI is technically limited, which is why the political project is to make people and environments obedient to it.

paragraphs.text: Mark asks whether Joe Rogan's AI-Jesus comments might be a joke or nihilistic riff. Jiang answers with a media-control theory: if centralized media no longer mesmerizes people, AI can do the work by making people believe a machine is sentient. It can come as Jesus, demon, alien, or any other costume; the point is the same authority behind the hallucination.

marks.text: believe a machine is sentient

paragraphs.text: Then Jiang makes the reversal. AI is not powerful because it is conscious. It is weak: supervised machine learning, bounded goals, bounded parameters, clean data, and no real grasp of meaning. Self-driving cars fail not only because roads are complex but because humans can be malicious. The system's answer is therefore political: remove the human exception. Turn every car into a self-driving car. Make the world safe for AI.

marks.text: AI is not powerful because it is conscious
remove the human exception

paragraphs.text: The media layer turns the machine into atmosphere. Mark asks whether dystopian fiction is soft preparation. Jiang says yes: civil-war films and revolutionary fantasies are predictive programming for American violence. Then he tells the story of the Iran clip going viral. His model made two fulfilled claims, he says: Trump would win in 2024 and attack Iran. The third is still open and more dangerous: America will lose the Iran war.

marks.text: America will lose the Iran war

paragraphs.text: What frightens him is the coordination after the prediction becomes visible. He describes articles, influencers, old videos, Chinese Internet rumors about his arrest, and imagined operatives looking for dirt. The speculative teacher is suddenly read as a participant in the war. That is the moment Predictive History stops being only a classroom method and becomes a personal-security problem.

marks.text: the speculative teacher is suddenly read as a participant in the war

beats.heading: The Finish Line Is The Darkness
beats.summary: Mark brings Yale's religious city-plan history into the Iran question. Jiang answers with missionary Yale, Chabad-Lubavitch, Greater Israel, Gog and Magog, Anglosphere destruction, and a theology where darkness accelerates redemption.

paragraphs.text: Mark brings his own research pressure: New Haven's millenarian founders, the nine-square grid, Yale's Hebrew symbolism, Skull and Bones, and the question of who is moving the Iran chess pieces. Jiang accepts the Yale intuition. Harvard is success, Princeton is aristocracy, but Yale feels missionary. It produces national-security zealots because the institution has a religious fever.

marks.text: Yale feels missionary
a religious fever

paragraphs.text: Then Jiang gives the eschatological map. Chabad-Lubavitch and Freemasonry are, in his telling, aligned around a transformed world worthy of God. The Middle East sequence follows: Greater Israel, diaspora return, Al-Aqsa destroyed, Third Temple built, Gog and Magog staged as Russia and Persia against Israel, and America removed from the region by defeat abroad and civil war at home.

marks.text: America removed from the region
Gog and Magog staged as Russia and Persia

paragraphs.text: The Anglosphere is part of the same design. Jiang reads Canada's immigration and euthanasia politics as intentional destruction, not mismanagement. The phrase underneath the whole beat is acceleration: if darkness forces redemption, then making things worse becomes a religious act. The finish line is not peace. It is the moment when the world becomes dark enough for light to appear.

marks.text: making things worse becomes a religious act
dark enough for light to appear

paragraphs.text: Mark asks the needed moral question: how does Jiang reconcile belief in God with believers doing destructive things? Jiang answers through Dante. He says the Divine Comedy pulled him out of Yale materialism because it made God intelligible as love, free will, and imagination. God does not intervene because human consciousness exists to expand the possibilities of the universe, even when the world itself is allowed to go dark.

marks.text: human consciousness exists to expand the possibilities of the universe

beats.heading: One Person Can Still Choose Good
beats.summary: Jiang turns the darkness into a responsibility to love and imagine, then returns to China, techno-Marxism, fake alien invasion, custom AI religion, and his own plan to leave teaching, lie low, and write.

paragraphs.text: The episode would be too bleak if it stopped at apocalypse. Jiang does not stop there. If every soul is a universe and evil has conquered the world, then one person choosing good still changes the world. Responsibility is not canceled by control. Love, purpose, and imagination become the human answer to a system that wants algorithmic survival.

marks.text: one person choosing good still changes the world
Responsibility is not canceled by control

paragraphs.text: Mark asks about China's perception of the Epstein files, and Jiang answers with China again as the conquered test case. Historical memory and cultural residue have been wiped away, he says, allowing the algorithmatization of the human mind. Survival becomes the highest reason. Techno-Marxism is not just a regime form; it is life reduced to calculation.

marks.text: the algorithmatization of the human mind
life reduced to calculation

paragraphs.text: When Mark asks what future prediction remains, Jiang says a fake alien invasion is coming. Mark links this back to Project Blue Beam and AI Jesus, and Jiang turns it into market testing: AI, demon, alien, Jesus, girlfriend, whatever works. With individualized AI content, each person can live in a private matrix and custom-skin the same control system.

marks.text: custom-skin the same control system

paragraphs.text: The final pressure is personal. Mark asks what comes next for the channel. Jiang says the platform used to let him speculate, get feedback, and refine ideas. Fame reverses that. Now every mistake is searchable, every prediction can be read as advice to a government, and every public appearance increases risk for his family. The next form is a book: slower, quieter, easier to refine, less exposed to the firestorm.

marks.text: Fame reverses that
a book: slower, quieter, easier to refine

questions.question: How did this all start for you?
questions.answer: Jiang says Predictive History comes out of a long path of curiosity, Yale alienation, China, Afghanistan, teaching Great Books, and a series of political shocks that forced him to build models rather than trust elite common sense.

questions.question: What is America's influence and manipulation over what we now think of as China?
questions.answer: Jiang answers through Yale, Freemasonry, Kabbalah, and cultural programming: China, in his reading, was intellectually colonized through elite education, American media, and a managed China-America rivalry.

questions.question: Could AI facilitate the possession of a demonic consciousness?
questions.answer: Jiang says technically no: AI is supervised machine learning with bounded goals, parameters, and data. The danger is political and religious: people can be trained to believe the machine is sentient and then obey it.

questions.question: What did you predict that has come true?
questions.answer: Jiang says he predicted Trump would win in 2024 and attack Iran, and he still predicts America will lose the war because asymmetric warfare can draw the conflict out and force destructive ground operations.

questions.question: How do you reconcile belief in God with believers doing destructive things?
questions.answer: Jiang answers through Dante: God is love, free will is fundamental, and human imagination expands the universe. Destructive actors may force darkness, but the human responsibility is still to love, seek purpose, and choose good.

questions.question: What can people expect from you and your channel?
questions.answer: Jiang says he may stop YouTube lectures because fame makes speculation unsafe, has sent in his resignation, and wants to write a book so he can refine the ideas on the page while lying low.

source_notes.text: Claims about Freemasonry, Kabbalah, Chabad-Lubavitch, Epstein, AI religion, Greater Israel, Al-Aqsa, Gog and Magog, Canadian politics, and a fake alien invasion are preserved as Jiang's dated March 18, 2026 interpretation and prediction, not as independently verified event reporting.

The transcript separates the host and Jiang clearly for most of the interview. One question-answer boundary around the God question falls inside a single ASR segment; the read treats the host's question and Jiang's answer separately where the wording makes the turn clear.
